definition: Sorting is the process of arranging data in a specific order such as ascending or descending.
Sorting organizes elements so they can be searched and processed efficiently.
Sorting helps improve the performance of many algorithms.

explanations: Bubble Sort repeatedly compares adjacent elements and swaps them if they are in the wrong order.
Bubble Sort moves the largest unsorted element to its correct position during each pass.
Bubble Sort is a simple comparison-based sorting algorithm suitable for learning purposes.
Selection Sort repeatedly selects the smallest element from the unsorted portion and places it at the beginning.
Selection Sort divides the array into sorted and unsorted regions.
Selection Sort reduces the unsorted section one element at a time.
sample_codes: for(int i=0;i<arr.length-1;i++){ int min=i; for(int j=i+1;j<arr.length;j++){ if(arr[j]<arr[min]) min=j; } int temp=arr[min]; arr[min]=arr[i]; arr[i]=temp; }

explanations: Insertion Sort builds a sorted array one element at a time.
Insertion Sort inserts each element into its correct position in the already sorted part.
Insertion Sort is efficient for small or nearly sorted datasets.
sample_codes: for(int i=1;i<arr.length;i++){ int key=arr[i]; int j=i-1; while(j>=0 && arr[j]>key){ arr[j+1]=arr[j]; j--; } arr[j+1]=key; }

explanations: Merge Sort uses divide and conquer to sort an array.
Merge Sort recursively splits the array and merges sorted halves.
Merge Sort is stable and performs well on large datasets.
sample_codes: void mergeSort(int[] arr,int l,int r){ if(l<r){ int m=(l+r)/2; mergeSort(arr,l,m); mergeSort(arr,m+1,r); merge(arr,l,m,r); } }

explanations: Quick Sort selects a pivot and partitions the array around it.
Quick Sort recursively sorts elements smaller and larger than the pivot.
Quick Sort is one of the fastest sorting algorithms in practice.
sample_codes: void quickSort(int[] arr,int low,int high){ if(low<high){ int pi=partition(arr,low,high); quickSort(arr,low,pi-1); quickSort(arr,pi+1,high); } }

definition: An array is a collection of elements stored in contiguous memory locations.
Arrays store multiple values of the same data type using indexes.
Arrays allow fast access to elements using their index.
facts: Arrays store elements in contiguous memory locations.
Arrays provide O(1) access using indexes.
Arrays are cache-friendly because of contiguous memory.
examples: Marks of students in a class

definition: A Linked List is a linear data structure where elements are stored in nodes connected by pointers.
A Linked List consists of nodes, where each node contains data and a reference to the next node.
Unlike arrays, linked list elements are not stored in contiguous memory locations.
facts: Linked Lists use dynamic memory allocation.
Insertion and deletion are efficient compared to arrays.
Linked Lists do not provide direct access using indexes.
Each node contains data and a link to another node.
The last node points to NULL in a singly linked list.
advantages: No memory wastage due to fixed size
Easy implementation of dynamic data structures

explanations: A Singly Linked List consists of nodes where each node stores data and a pointer to the next node.
Traversal in a Singly Linked List is possible only in the forward direction.
The last node of a Singly Linked List points to NULL.
A Doubly Linked List contains two pointers in each node: one to the next node and one to the previous node.
It allows traversal in both forward and backward directions.
Doubly Linked Lists require extra memory because each node stores an additional pointer.
In a Circular Linked List, the last node points back to the first node instead of NULL.
The nodes form a circular structure that can be traversed continuously.
Circular Linked Lists are commonly used in round-robin scheduling.
A Circular Doubly Linked List combines the features of circular and doubly linked lists.
Each node contains pointers to both the next and previous nodes.
The last node connects back to the first node, creating a circular structure.

traversal: Visit each node from head to tail.
searching: Sequentially compare each node until the target value is found.
